title: Data Structures
purpose: Choose representations that make required operations correct and efficient.
outcomes: Explain Data Structures concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important Data Structures features
Implement, test and debug practical Data Structures solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of Data Structures before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: Complexity and memory
topics.definition: A data structure organizes values so required operations have predictable time and space costs.
topics.whyItMatters: The same data can be represented in ways that make lookup, insertion, ordering or traversal dramatically cheaper or more expensive.
whenToUse: Choose arrays for compact indexed access
Choose hashing for fast average membership or lookup
Choose trees or heaps when order, hierarchy or priority drives operations
whenNotToUse: Selecting from habit rather than operation requirements
Ignoring memory overhead and input limits
Using a sophisticated structure before a simple one fails constraints
internalWorking: Representation determines which locations must be visited for each operation
Contiguous storage improves cache locality
Pointer-based structures allow flexible links but add indirection
Invariants such as heap order or tree balance preserve complexity guarantees
tradeoffs: Fast average behavior versus stronger worst-case guarantees
commonMistakes: Quoting complexity without defining the operation
topics.practicalExample: Use a queue for breadth-first prerequisite traversal, a set for visited skills and a map from skill to dependent roadmap nodes.

topics.title: Hash tables
topics.definition: Collections are data structures with different access and ordering guarantees. A List preserves sequence, a Set enforces uniqueness, and a Map associates unique keys with values.
topics.whyItMatters: Choosing the correct collection makes required operations clear and avoids accidental performance or correctness problems.
whenToUse: Use a List for ordered values and index-based traversal
Use a Set for membership checks and uniqueness
Use a Map for key-based lookup, grouping and counting
whenNotToUse: Using a List for frequent membership checks over large data
Using a hash collection when sorted iteration is required
Using mutable keys whose equality or hash can change after insertion
internalWorking: A hash map computes a hash, maps it to a bucket and compares candidate keys for equality
Collisions place multiple keys in the same bucket and are resolved by chained or tree-like structures
Resizing allocates a larger table and redistributes entries
Correct lookup requires equals and hashCode to obey the same equality contract
tradeoffs: Hash collections offer average constant-time access but no natural sort order
Tree collections provide ordering with logarithmic operations
Array-backed lists give fast indexing while linked structures favor specific insertion patterns
commonMistakes: Choosing LinkedList for general-purpose performance without measuring
topics.practicalExample: Use Map<sectionId, List<Student>> to group students by section and a Set<String> to reject duplicate college email addresses.

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of Hash tables.
interviewQuestions.answer: I choose a collection from its required operations and guarantees. HashMap gives average O(1) lookup by hashing a key into a bucket and checking equality within collisions. Its key contract and resize behavior matter, while TreeMap is preferable when sorted keys are required.

topics.title: Hash tables: applied patterns
topics.definition: Collections are data structures with different access and ordering guarantees. A List preserves sequence, a Set enforces uniqueness, and a Map associates unique keys with values.
topics.whyItMatters: Choosing the correct collection makes required operations clear and avoids accidental performance or correctness problems.
whenToUse: Use a List for ordered values and index-based traversal
Use a Set for membership checks and uniqueness
Use a Map for key-based lookup, grouping and counting
whenNotToUse: Using a List for frequent membership checks over large data
Using a hash collection when sorted iteration is required
Using mutable keys whose equality or hash can change after insertion
internalWorking: A hash map computes a hash, maps it to a bucket and compares candidate keys for equality
Collisions place multiple keys in the same bucket and are resolved by chained or tree-like structures
Resizing allocates a larger table and redistributes entries
Correct lookup requires equals and hashCode to obey the same equality contract
tradeoffs: Hash collections offer average constant-time access but no natural sort order
Tree collections provide ordering with logarithmic operations
Array-backed lists give fast indexing while linked structures favor specific insertion patterns
commonMistakes: Choosing LinkedList for general-purpose performance without measuring
topics.practicalExample: Use Map<sectionId, List<Student>> to group students by section and a Set<String> to reject duplicate college email addresses.

topics.title: Cache-aware layout
topics.definition: CSS is a rule system that resolves the cascade, computes values, lays out boxes and paints them into composited layers.
topics.whyItMatters: It separates content structure from responsive visual presentation across screen sizes, input modes and user preferences.
whenToUse: Use Grid for two-dimensional page relationships
Use Flexbox for one-dimensional alignment and distribution
Use intrinsic sizing, minmax and wrapping for resilient components
whenNotToUse: Fixed heights for variable text content
Increasing specificity to fight unclear ownership
Viewport-width font scaling that makes text unpredictable
internalWorking: Selectors match elements and the cascade resolves competing declarations
Computed styles feed layout algorithms
Layout produces geometry, paint creates visual layers and compositing combines them
Some properties trigger layout and paint while transform and opacity are often composited
tradeoffs: Utility classes speed consistent delivery but require conventions
Component scoping reduces leakage but may duplicate tokens
Animation improves feedback but can harm performance or motion-sensitive users
commonMistakes: Missing min-width: 0 on grid or flex children
Hiding overflow instead of fixing the sizing cause
topics.practicalExample: Use grid-template-columns: minmax(0,1fr) 18rem and collapse to one column at the content breakpoint; allow long labels to wrap without changing control dimensions.

topics.title: Concurrency concerns
topics.definition: Concurrency coordinates multiple tasks whose lifetimes overlap. Parallelism executes work simultaneously; asynchronous I/O lets a task yield while waiting for external work.
topics.whyItMatters: Applications must remain responsive while handling slow networks, files, user requests and CPU work without corrupting shared state.
whenToUse: Use async I/O for many independent network or disk waits
Use bounded worker pools for controlled parallel work
Protect shared mutable state with ownership, immutability or synchronization
whenNotToUse: Adding threads to CPU work without measuring cores and overhead
Blocking an event-loop thread
Holding a lock while performing slow external I/O
internalWorking: A scheduler chooses which runnable task gets CPU time
Async runtimes register I/O readiness and resume continuations later
Threads have independent stacks but share process memory
Synchronization establishes ordering and visibility for shared state
tradeoffs: More throughput versus scheduling and coordination overhead
Shared memory is fast but increases race risk
Async code scales I/O waits but can make cancellation and error propagation harder
commonMistakes: Assuming a single statement is automatically thread-safe
topics.practicalExample: Fetch independent question sets concurrently with a timeout, but limit concurrency so one user cannot exhaust database or network connections.
